entry.title: Re: Extract a measure depending on a max measure
entry.content:
No problem 🙂

I can't find the "Period" column in the screenshot you shared, but I assume it's on a day-level.

You can add "Period" (or CALDAY or whatever your period column is called) to the window function:

case 
    when qta colli = MAX(qta colli) OVER (PARTITION by Centro, Codice Materiale, Period) then 
        tempo lordo lavorato
    else 
        0
    end

Which should do the trick.

The window function, such as MAX(), will use the "Partition" clause to calculate the value. 
But(!) it's important to note that all columns not in the Partition will be ignored. 

So, if you add your period - it should all be good 🙂

entry.title: What's New in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 Analytics - January 2026
entry.content:
As part of our monthly releases during Q4 2025, we have introduced a new set of analytics innovations across SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management. These enhancements reflect our continued investment in scalable, global analytics and address some of the most frequently requested capabilities from our customers.

The Q4 focus was clear: extend analytics beyond a single product, enable global usability, and empower organizations with more flexible, enterprise-grade insights. This release delivers on that vision with three key innovations:

  • Metadata Translation Support for Embedded SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Stories
  • Currency Conversion 1.0
  • Live Integration with SAP Analytics Cloud, Enterprise Edition (Restricted GA)

Below is a closer look at how each of these enhancements delivers tangible business value.

Metadata Translation Support for Embedded SAC Stories

SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management are deployed globally, supporting users across regions, languages, and cultures. To ensure consistent and localized user experiences, both solutions offer Language Adaptation, enabling administrators to translate field labels, code lists, UI texts, and custom messages.

Until now, Embedded SAC supported data translations and translations created directly within SAC’s Query Designer and Story Designer. With the November 2025 release, metadata translations maintained centrally in the Language Adaptation tool are now automatically applied to Embedded SAC Stories.

Business impact:

  • Single point of translation maintenance across operational UIs and analytics
  • Consistent multilingual experiences for business users
  • Reduced administrative overhead by eliminating duplicate translation efforts

All translated field-related metadata is seamlessly reflected in the Embedded SAC user interface, ensuring analytics remain as intuitive and localized as the underlying applications.

You can see how this works in this video.

Currency Conversion 1.0

For organizations operating across multiple regions, currency conversion is a foundational analytics requirement—particularly in sales and service reporting. With the December 2025 release, we introduce Currency Conversion 1.0 for Embedded SAC.

Administrators can now define a target currency for amount-based attributes:

  • During data modeling in the Query Designer
  • Directly in the Story Designer for specific charts or tables

Different target currencies can be applied within the same story, offering greater flexibility for global and regional reporting.

Key capabilities and considerations:

  • Centralized rate maintenance: Conversion rates maintained in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 or SAP Enterprise Service Management are automatically used in SAC stories.
  • Automatic rate selection: Conversion rates are applied at runtime; users do not manage conversion datasets manually.
  • Modeling requirement: Both amount and currency attributes must be included in the data source for conversion to work correctly.

This capability enables consistent, accurate financial reporting while preserving a single source of truth for currency data. Stay tuned for the ability to select a currency conversion date as the next enhancement to this feature!

You can see how this works in this video.

Live Integration with SAP Analytics Cloud, Enterprise Edition (Restricted GA)

Modern enterprises rely on multiple SAP solutions—such as SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 for CRM and SAP SuccessFactors for HCM—and require a unified analytics layer across systems. SAP Analytics Cloud, Enterprise Edition fulfills this role.

With the December 2025 release, SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management can now integrate with Enterprise SAC via live connections.

Key benefits of live integration:

  • Real-time insights: Data is accessed directly from the source systems during story execution.
  • Enhanced data security: Data remains within the source system and is discarded once the story is closed.
  • Complete data access: All available data sources in Sales & Service Cloud V2 and Enterprise Service Management are accessible in Enterprise SAC.
  • Advanced analytics and modeling: Leverage Enterprise SAC capabilities to further model and visualize operational data.

This feature is currently available under restricted general availability. Customers interested in early access can request enablement by creating a case under component CEC-CRM-ANA. Broader availability is planned in the near future.

You can see how this works in this video.

Help documentation from SAP Analytics Cloud is available for this as well & can be accessed here & here.

Enabling Truly Global, Enterprise-Grade Analytics

Together, these enhancements significantly strengthen the analytics foundation of SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management. By improving multilingual support, enabling multi-currency reporting, and unlocking enterprise-wide analytics through live integration, we are helping customers scale insights globally—without compromising consistency, security, or performance.

These innovations represent another step toward delivering analytics that are not only powerful, but truly global.

entry.title: Re: WM Monitor queues WMQF* state SYSFAIL with no informa...
entry.content:
Good day @isolde_grammfe1fd5 

Thank you for your question.

What you described is a typical EWM QM processing issue.

I suggest you first identify what the queue is trying to execute.

Open your Warehouse Monitor, tools --> Message Queue.

Find the stuck queue and execute

Open the message details for that queue entry. Note the details.

The error message “No display program registered for this queue” Message no. /SCWM/MONITOR055” means that the monitor cannot jump to a dedicated display program for that queue prefix. It does not mean there is technically an error. You should still find out the trouble for WMQF.

As described in KBA 3301813, this is mostly caused by a few factors:

  • Your storage type setting blocks Q unrestricted posting. So, check your EWM customizing. Particularly Maintain Storage Types -->  Putaway Control. If the destination storage type is used for Q to unrestricted postings, then ensure you did not set “No Mixed Stock Types”.
  • Check your Putaway strategy and destination storage type determination.
  • Your stock may be blocked by an open task in your warehouse or bin is blocked.
  • Check Maintain Follow Up Actions app, verify the HU WT related flags are consistent with your process.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: calculate using actual overhead costs in actual cost...
entry.content:
Good day @makoto_kasuya91 

Thank you for your question.

You do not use standard cost estimate logic to calculate actual overhead in public cloud. You make use of Actual Overhead with a costing sheet. Thereafter, you initiate Run Overhead Calculation – Actual during period end. You can also schedule this. See Overhead Calculation for more details.

In your setup can you confirm you have:

  1. Maintained Costing sheet for actual overhead (base, rate, credit key)?
  2. Have you assigned the costing sheet to the cost object you want to settle?
  3. Does the actual cost basis exist?

If the answer to those questions is yes, then you can go ahead as follows:

Calculate actual overhead manually by opening Run Overhead Calcualtion -Actual app. Select your object type and execute the run. You can review the postings

Alternatively, you can schedule it as a job. See Actual Overhead Calculation for Projects and WBS Elements for more details.

Please note, if you are using Actual Costing/Material Ledger actual cost, overhead must be first be posted as actual overhead to the cost objects, then it becomes part of the actual cost picture  at the end of the period.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Understanding the New Tags in the SAF-T Poland VAT Report (JPK_V7M(3) and JPK_V7K(3))
entry.content:
Poland's tax reporting landscape is evolving, and SAP has introduced new tags in the Standard Audit File for Tax (SAF-T) structures JPK_V7M(3) and JPK_V7K(3) to help businesses stay compliant with the country's electronic invoicingrequirements. 

Understanding the Four New Tags 

In this blog article, we' ll explain what the new tags mean and how to use them, along with examples to make things clearer. 

The new structures JPK_V7M(3) and JPK_V7K(3) of the Polish SAF-T VAT report now include the following tags designed to simplify the reporting process: 

  • "NrKSeF": This tag is the KSeF Reference Number, which serves as an identifier for invoices in the National e-Invoice System (KSeF).  
  • "OFF": Use this tag for invoices referred to in Article 106nf(1) of the act that don't have a KSeF identification number and haven't been submitted to KSeF.  
  • "BFK": This applies to either electronic or paper invoices not categorized as e-invoices by KSeF, for example in cases where it’s not mandatory to issue invoices using the National e-Invoice System.  
  • "DI": Represents any proof of transaction other than an invoice issued through the KSeF. This applies to all other cases of documents that will not have a KSeF number, have not been sent to KSeF and can’t be categorized as ‘BFK’ or ‘OFF’.

Examples 

To help companies meet the new standards, let's look at examples of where these tags are applicable. 

The ‘NrKSeF’ Tag 

Use the "NrKSeF" tag when there's a KSeF Reference Number for an invoice. This number identifies the invoice within the National e-Invoice System. For example, if you're processing an invoice registered in KSeF, you should tag it with the appropriate "NrKSeF" number. 

Complete this field if, as of the date of submission of JPK_VAT, the invoice has been assigned a KSeF number. 

The ‘OFF’ Tag 

Apply the "OFF" tag to invoices referred to in Article 106nf(1) of the act that don’t have a KSeF number as of the date of submission of the records.  

This designation applies to invoices issued during the period of malfunction of the National e-Invoice System, according to Art.106nf(1). This designation indicates that, due to the inactivity of this system, the invoice does not have a KSeF identifying number as of the date of submission of the JPK_VAT. 

If an invoice exists in e-Document processing but still lacks a KSeF Reference Number, mark it as "OFF". 

The ‘BFK’ Tag 

The "BFK" tag is for invoices not handled by KSeF, such as paper invoices, vendor invoices for foreign transactions or invoices with document types that are excluded from KSeF. This category also includes invoices from cash registers, invoices from consumers in B2C relations issued outside of KSeF, invoices for financial services using KSeF exemption, etc.  

This tag allows you to indicate transactions that for various legal reasons fall outside of the KSeF.  

If the document is not marked with DI, OFF or KSeF th en it is reported by JPK_V7K(3) and JPK_V7M(3) with status BFK.

The ‘DI’ Tag 

Use the "DI" tag for all other documents that serve as transaction proofs but are not an invoice and do not qualify as ‘OFF’ or ‘BFK’.  

For example, daily reports from cash registers, internal documents, customs documents, or other accounting documents.  

Invoices issued in the offline24 mode must also be tagged with the ‘DI’ tag.  

DI should be also assigned to invoices, which should be registered in KSeF, but due to different reasons cannot be registered. This may happen in the initial period applicable with KSeF, when for various reasons, mainly technological and configuration ones, the taxpayer is unable to register the invoice in KSeF, but will have to forward it to the business partner. 

With SAP Document and Reporting Compliance you can select for which type of transaction DI is relevant. See the example below. 

Why This Matters

These tags help you accurately categorize your transactions, ensuring compliance with Polish tax regulations while reducing reporting errors.  

You can also find detailed information about the changes in the JPK_V7M(3) and JPK_V7K(3) structures in the Regulatory Change Manager. 

For detailed guidance on implementing KSeF requirements in your SAP system, check out the related blog articles. 

  • How Official Document Numbering helps to fulfill the Polish KSeF Requirements in S4HANA Public Cloud 
  • How you can manage Polish KSeF Requirement in SAP S/⁠4HANA Cloud Private Edition & SAP S/⁠4HANA 
  • How to store long Supplier Invoice Reference Numbers in SAP S/4HANA ERP Products 

 Staying updated with the latest legal changes is essential for navigating the ever-evolving taxation landscape. By understanding how to use these tags, you can ensure compliance with national regulations, reduce errors, and streamline your tax processes.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting - Using Automatic Posting Rules for Consolidation of Investments
entry.content:
Hi Robert,

Just to be sure we are on the same page...

1) As of release 2602 it is possible to create custome consolidation methods. This is mentioned in this blog. I have now added a link to the documentation of this topic.

2) The group structure filters can be used for consolidation units, partner units, and investee units. How you apply the filter is defined in the rule. Please check the example in this blog: the rule CI_10_01_1 will filter the data where consolidation units are consolidated with methods parent or purchase, and the partner units are consolidated with purchase (first consolidation only).  This provides high flexibility in filtering units based on their assignment in the groups. 

Best regards,

Grégoire

entry.title: Gamify Your Classroom with Business Builders —powered by SAP Analytics Cloud; designed for educators
entry.content:
In a world powered by AI and data, students need more than technical skills—they need to think critically, communicate clearly, and make decisions based on data. Business Builders helps educators bring these skills to life through engaging, scenario-based games—powered by SAP Analytics Cloud and designed by educators for educators.

What is Business Builders?

Business Builders is a free, cloud-based platform that turns data literacy and business storytelling into an interactive learning experience. Students explore real-world scenarios, use SAP Analytics Cloud to analyze data, and compete on leaderboards—earning instant feedback and celebrating success.

Each game challenges students to:

  • Visualize and interpret real business data
  • Answer scenario-specific questions
  • Climb the leaderboard through accurate, timely analysis

Scenarios range from foundational business skills to advanced decision-making—ideal for classrooms focused on analytics, strategy, finance, IT, sustainability, and more.

Who is it for?

Business Builders is built for higher education instructors across all academic levels and disciplines. Whether you teach intro-level undergrads or MBA students, the platform adapts to your classroom.

  • No cost for educators or students
  • No software installation required
  • Built-in gamification to boost engagement
  • Easy setup of students via instructor invitation links

What's in it for your students?

Students will:

  • Learn data analysis and visualization with SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Develop analytical thinking and problem-solving skills as well as data storytelling
  • Collaborate and compete in a game-based environment
  • Build job-ready capabilities for internships and careers

Available Game Scenarios

  • Supply Chain Management
  • International Expansion
  • Sustainability Portfolio
  • NEW: The Maple Heir (Business Processes Micro-Challenges)
  • NEW: The Agent

Are you a professor/ lecturer or you would love to play Business Builders with students? Check out our BusinessBuilders and register now for a free instructor account! OR in case you have some questions, check out our materials, and one-pager in our Digital Library - Business Builders 

Any questions? Write to universityalliances@sap.com 

 

We look so much forward to hearing from you and your experience with Business Builders! 

Find here below a sample question for your reference

entry.title: Reporting Actual Working Time When Breaks Are Recorded in Different Ways
entry.content:
The approach is not perfect (≈98% accurate) and works only if cancelled timesheets are excluded (see my last post on timesheet integration for details).  Before we start with the report story, we first need to understand what kind of break types are possible in time tracking. 

 Breaks can appear in timesheets in three ways:

  • Separate break entry (non-overlapping)
    A break is recorded with the time type “Break” between two working-time entries.
  • Separate break entry (overlapping)
    A break is recorded with the time type “Break” but overlaps with a working-time entry.
  • Implicit break
    No break is recorded explicitly; instead, there is a gap between two working-time entries.

Now that we know all the ways breaks can be recorded, lets create the report. For better understanding I identified four different steps to be taken:

  1. Collect all relevant timesheet entries.
  2. Create calculated columns to:
    • Sum working time
    • Sum break time
    • Count working-time entries
  3. Build a report table.
  4. Calculate actual working time based on the number of working-time entries per day.

 

1.     Collect all relevant timesheet entries.

Start from the Employee Timesheet domain and add Employee Timesheet Entry as well as Employee Time and Time Type. Join additional domains as needed (via User or Job Info—either is fine). 

In terms of fields make sure to select all the relevant date fields and the number of hours from the Employee Time entity as well as the external code from the time type entity. Everything else can be selected as you need it.

Now add a filter to the selected data base and filter the timesheet approval status to:

  • Approved
  • To Be Approved
  • To Be Submitted

Exclude cancelled entries .We don't want the cancelled entries in the report as they will generate duplicates. If you are not sure why, I have written a time tracking integration blogpost in which i explain how the timesheet audit is set up.

 

2.     Required Measures

Now for the calculations. Create the following calculated measures (all aggregated with SUM)

 

Brutto Working Time (“Bruttoarbeitszeit”)
Break ("Pause")
Number of Timesheet Entries (“Anzahl Timesheeteinträge”)
Sum of working-time hours onlySum of break hours only

Counts how many working-time entries exist per day. This measure is later aggregated in the report story.

Assumption: If there is only one working-time entry, the break is likely recorded separately using a break time type.

If (time type code="working time",Number of hours,0)

If (time type code="break",Number of hours,0)

 If( time type code= "working time",1,0)

 

 3.    Create the cross-tab table report

We have everything we need in order to create a cross-tab table and report on the actual working time. Select all of the columns you need. You can select the calculated columns to be displayed if you want to, but this is not mandatory.

  

 

4.    Calculate actual working time  - where the magic happens

 Create a calculated story measure that adjusts working time based on how breaks are recorded:

IF( Number of timesheetentries=1, Brutto working time - Break,Brutto working time)

 Logic: If there is only one working-time entry, subtract the explicitly recorded break. Else If there are multiple working-time entries, I assume breaks are already implicit.

 

 

 And voilà -  add the calculation as a measure to your table report 🙂

Now you can correctly report on only working time regardless of the break type used.

Conclusion

When you link the break deduction calculation to the amount of timesheet entries you can almost accurately report on only working time regardless of the break types used. For me, this approach only worked using a report story even though I appreciate a good old canvas just as much.

entry.title: Re: Stock Transfer Order Delivery completed indicator
entry.content:
Hello @Pdud_97 

You requirement would be quite easy to implement on-premise or in private cloud. Just build a simple report selecting undelivered STOs in a give time frame and updating the STOs with "delivery completed". Then run the report as a periodic background job - easy-peasy.

Public cloud is much more restrictive in terms of custom enhancements. You could build a side-by-side enhancement on SAP BTP and update the STOs with API_STOCKTRANSPORTORDER (SAP Help: Stock Transport Order (OData V4) ). 

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Adding a Column in SNC portal->Release Process->Overview Tab
entry.content:
Hello Technical Folks, 

I need to add APN column under the SNC Portal(Supplier Network Collaboration) under the Release Process->Overview tab in SPP system.

I found 2 BADI's namely /SCF/RELEASE_UI to backend logic to fetch data from DB table and populate the Structure, and WD_BADI_DOMODIFYVIEW to add the Column in the UI page. 

As far i am able to inject the data in backend and I was able to add the column in the Modify view BADI.

There 21 pre-designed columns in the screen, while injecting the APN column into UI, i can see differences of Property Bindings are not added to my Column. And i am finding it difficult to add those required Bindings. Please help me on how to add property Bindings for the column. TIA.

Method /SCF/IF_EX_RELEASE_UI~LOAD_OVERVIEW_TBV

  METHOD /scf/if_ex_release_ui~load_overview_tbv.

    TYPES : BEGIN OF ty_matnr,
              matnr TYPE /sapapo/matnr,
            END OF ty_matnr.

    DATA: ls_tbvrowvalues TYPE /scf/stdtbvvalues_str,
          it_matnr        TYPE TABLE OF ty_matnr.

    CONSTANTS : lc_ricefw       TYPE string VALUE 'SNC_REL',
                lc_obj          TYPE string VALUE 'ZSPP_SNC_REL_OVERVIEW',
                lc_en           TYPE c LENGTH 2 VALUE 'EN',
                lc_custmaterial TYPE string VALUE 'CUSTMATERIAL',
                lc_apn          TYPE c LENGTH 3 VALUE 'APN'.

    SELECT SINGLE FROM ztall_enh_switch
      FIELDS active
      WHERE ricefw_id = @LC_ricefw
        AND object_name = @LC_obj
      INTO @DATA(zlv_act).

    IF zlv_act IS NOT INITIAL.
      it_matnr = VALUE #( BASE it_matnr
                          FOR <lfs_ovw> IN ct_tbvrowdata
                          ( matnr = VALUE #( <lfs_ovw>-rowvalues[ colid = lc_custmaterial ]-cellvalues OPTIONAL ) )
                         ).

      SORT it_matnr BY matnr ASCENDING.
      DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM it_matnr COMPARING matnr.

      SELECT FROM /sapapo/matapn AS apn
        INNER JOIN /sapapo/matkey AS matkey ON apn~matid = matkey~matid
        FIELDS matnr, apn~altpn
        FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @IT_matnr
        WHERE matnr = @IT_matnr-matnr AND apn~apntype = @LC_en
        INTO TABLE @DATA(it_apn).

      LOOP AT ct_tbvrowdata ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_row>).
        ls_tbvrowvalues-colid = lc_apn.
        ls_tbvrowvalues-cellvalues = VALUE #( it_apn[
                                      matnr = VALUE #( <lfs_row>-rowvalues[ colid = lc_custmaterial ]-cellvalues OPTIONAL )
                                                    ]-altpn OPTIONAL ).
        INSERT ls_tbvrowvalues INTO TABLE <lfs_row>-rowvalues.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

METHOD IF_WD_BADI_DOMODIFYVIEW~WDDOMODIFYVIEW:

  METHOD if_wd_badi_domodifyview~wddomodifyview.
    DATA: lo_table       TYPE REF TO cl_wd_table,
          lo_column      TYPE REF TO cl_wd_table_column,
          lo_caption     TYPE REF TO cl_wd_caption,
          lo_cell_editor TYPE REF TO cl_wd_input_field,
          lo_uielement   TYPE REF TO if_wd_view_element, "TYPE REF TO cl_wd_uielement,
          lv_view_id     TYPE string.
    TRY.
        lo_table ?= view->get_element( id = 'SALV_WD_UIE_TABLE' ).

        DATA: lt_columns TYPE cl_wd_abstr_table_column=>tt_abstr_table_column.
        IF lo_table IS BOUND.
          lt_columns = lo_table->get_columns( ).
        ENDIF.

        " Create the new column for APN
        lo_column = cl_wd_table_column=>new_table_column(
          id = 'APN'                           " Column ID
        ).

        " Create and configure the caption
        lo_caption = cl_wd_caption=>new_caption(
          id   = 'APN_CAPTION'
          text = 'APN'                         " Column header text
        ).

        " Set the caption for the column
        lo_column->set_header( lo_caption ).

        " Create the cell editor (Input Field)
        lo_cell_editor = cl_wd_input_field=>new_input_field(
          id       = 'APN_SALV_WD_CE'
          read_only = abap_true                " Set to abap_false if editable
        ).

        " Bind the cell editor to the context attribute
        " The binding path should match your context node structure
        " Typically: <NODE_NAME>.<ATTRIBUTE_NAME>
        lo_cell_editor->bind_value(
          path = 'APN'                         " Context attribute name
        ).

        " Set the cell editor for the column
        lo_column->set_table_cell_editor( the_table_cell_editor = lo_cell_editor ).

        " Set column properties
        lo_column->set_width( '100px' ).  " Adjust width as needed

        " Optional: Set column to be sortable
        lo_column->set_sort_state( value = 01 ).

        " Optional: Set column to be filterable
        lo_column->set_filter_value( value = '01' ).

        lo_column->set_visible( value = 02 ).

        lo_column->bind_accessibility_descr( path = 'APN' ).


        IF lo_table IS BOUND.
          lo_table->add_column(
            EXPORTING
              index      = 22
              the_column = lo_column
          ).
        ENDIF.

      CATCH cx_wd_general INTO DATA(lx_error).
        " Handle exception - log error or raise message
        DATA(lv_error_text) = lx_error->get_text( ).
        " You can add error handling logic here
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.

entry.title: Re: Exclude Billing Document from Scheduling Job if Alloc...
entry.content: In Public Cloud, you cannot filter the job directly; instead, use Output Parameter Determination (OPD) or a released BADI for output control to suppress output type assignment when Allocation Number is missing, thereby excluding the billing document from E-Invoicing Cockpit.

entry.title: Re: Bank transactions going to wrong account -error "Acco...
entry.content:
Good day @kezelda 

Thank you for your question.

That error indicates that the system is trying to post or clear against a vendor in your case K while the posting rule uses DZ. So, the error is related to the wrong auto allocation. The document type DZ is usually configured for Customer incoming payments, which is account type D. KBA 3431169 and 3473249 highlights these clearly.

Even if your log does not show the algorithm name, the system is still doing interpretation, for example based on a reference field like XBLNR, end-to-end ID, etc. So, if the algorithm picks the wrong vendor, then it will wrongly assign or the posting will fail because the doc type does not match.

Option fix 1: You should control this issue via Posting Rules and not via on premise transactions. You can fix the document type used in the Posting Rule via your CBC.  See Define and Assign Posting Rules for more insights.

Note that it is not always possible and KBA 3501569 highlights scenarios where this is the case.

Option fix 2: Find the root cause why your system determines the wrong BP. Check your parameter restrictions like XBLNR interval, your search mapping (maybe not specific enough), interpretation Algorithm (check for similar reference patterns for your customers and vendors). See KBA 3431169 for more insights.

Consider stabilizing your system by:

  • Ensuring your bank statement reference includes a unique identifier.
  • Tighten your search mappings and strings to avoid overlapping of vendor and customer.
  • If you have both AR and AP in the same bank statement format, ensure their external posting rules are not same.

You can validate by using Reprocess Bank Statements Items ap to open the item and see which account the system proposes, then simulate or post after you have corrected the posting rule and interpretation setup.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Customer Name range
entry.content:
Hi,

Check this:

/*SELECT * FROM "OCRD" T0*/

Declare FromCardName VARCHAR(254);
Declare ToCardName VARCHAR(254);

FromCardName := /* T0."CardName" */ '[%0]';
ToCardName := /* T0."CardName" */ '[%1]';

/*SELECT * FROM "@A_SUBDIV" T1*/

Declare FromSGrp VARCHAR(254);
Declare ToSGrp VARCHAR(254);

FromSGrp := /* T1."Code" */ '[%2]';
ToSGrp := /* T1."Code" */ '[%3]';

SELECT T0."DocNum", T0."DocDate", T0."CardCode", T0."CardName", T0."DocCur",
T3."ItmsGrpNam",
T5."Name" AS "Sub Product Group",
T4."Name",
'$' || T1."ItemCode" AS "Item Code",
T1."Dscription",
T1."WhsCode",
T1."Quantity" AS "PO Qty",
T1."OpenQty",
T1."unitMsr" as "UOM",
T1."PriceBefDi" AS "Unit Price",
T1."DiscPrcnt" AS "%Disc",
T1."TotalFrgn" AS "Total Order Value",
T0."U_ETA",
T0."U_SHIPMODE",
T0."U_REASON"
FROM OPOR T0
INNER JOIN POR1 T1 ON T0."DocEntry" = T1."DocEntry"
INNER JOIN OITM T2 ON T1."ItemCode" = T2."ItemCode"
INNER JOIN OITB T3 ON T2."ItmsGrpCod" = T3."ItmsGrpCod"
LEFT JOIN "@A_ITEMTYPE" T4 ON T2."U_ITEM_TYPES" = T4."Code"
LEFT OUTER JOIN "@A_SUBDIV" T5 ON T2."U_SUBDIV" = T5."Code"
WHERE T0."DocStatus" = 'O'
AND T1."OpenQty" <> '0'
AND (
(UPPER(T0."CardName") >= UPPER(FromCardName) OR FromCardName = '')
AND (UPPER(T0."CardName") <= UPPER(ToCardName) OR ToCardName = '')
)
AND (
(UPPER(IFNULL(T5."Code",'ZNA')) >= UPPER(FromSGrp) OR FromSGrp = '')
AND (UPPER(IFNULL(T5."Code",'ZNA')) <= UPPER(ToSGrp) OR ToSGrp = '')
);

Kr,

Jitin

entry.title: Re: How do you access country-dependent fields in BP mast...
entry.content:
You can find the Country Code in these CDS:

I_BUSINESSPARTNERCUSTOMER
I_BUSINESSPARTNERSUPPLIER 

Searching by BUSINESSPARTNER, fetch the field COUNTRY. 

These CDS can be used in Launchpad and ADT without problems.

Hi,

Giorgio

entry.title: SAP Build Code – Technical Series – Feature Flags Service
entry.content:
The landscape of application development on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) has evolved significantly. With SAP Build Code, developers have a unified, AI-powered environment specifically tailored for SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), SAP Fiori, mobile, and SAPUI5 development.

However, SAP Build Code is more than just a IDE or a single tool. It is a cohesive bundle of services that streamlines the entire application lifecycle. From coding and testing to deployment and management. For many developers, the challenge isn't just learning how to write code; it is understanding how these various interconnected services fit together to create enterprise-grade solutions.

I am launching this blog series to demystify the different services around SAP Build Code offering. We will visit each service individually to understand its specific role, how it integrates with the core development environment, and why it matters for your projects.

Chapter 1: Features Flag Service

In traditional application development, "deploying code" and "releasing a feature" were often the same event. If you pushed code to production, the feature was live. If something broke, you had to scramble to rollback the deployment or push a hotfix.

As we move into the world of cloud-native development on SAP BTP, we need to separate these two concepts. This is where the SAP Feature Flag Service becomes essential. It provides the ability to toggle functionality on or off without changing a single line of code or restarting your application.

In this installment of these SAP Build Code series, we explore how Feature Flags can help in your application's rollout strategy.

What is the SAP Feature Flag Service?

At its core, the SAP Feature Flag service is a control mechanism that allows you to enable or disable new features at runtime.

The critical distinction here is runtime. Unlike configuration changes that might require a restart, feature flags happen instantly. This capability allows you to:

  • Control Code Delivery: Merge code into the main branch without exposing it to users immediately.
  • Synchronize Rollouts: Coordinate the release of a feature that spans multiple microservices.
  • Fast Rollback: If a bug is discovered in a new feature, you can disable it instantly via the dashboard rather than redeploying a previous version of the application.

Why is it critical for cloud native application scenarios? Because there is a specific challenge in cloud development: Synchronization.

In a monolithic application, you deploy everything at once. In a microservice architecture (common in projects on BTP), a single business process might rely on three different services running in separate containers.

  • Scenario: Service A is updated with new logic, but Service B isn't ready yet.
  • Without Flags: You have to hold back the deployment of Service A.
  • With Flags: You deploy Service A with the feature toggled "Off." Once Service B is deployed and agreed that it is ready for end users to be used, you flip the switch, releasing the feature across the entire landscape simultaneously.

Knowing what a feature flag does is simple: it is an on/off switch. However, knowing how to use that switch to improve your software delivery lifecycle is where the real value lies.

Before we dive into the code implementation, it is crucial to understand the different delivery strategies available. These techniques allow you to move from a "Big Bang" deployment model to a more nuanced, risk-averse approach suitable for modern enterprise environments on SAP BTP.

Here are the four key delivery techniques you can leverage:

1. Latent Code Delivery

This technique involves deploying fully functional code to production but keeping it hidden from the end-user.

How it works:

  • You implement a toggle point in your application code that wraps the new functionality.
  • This toggle point is associated with an inactive feature flag.
  • The code resides in the production environment, ready to go, but remains dormant until you decide to switch the flag on at runtime.

Why use it? The primary benefit here is risk reduction. The development and delivery of new features do not put the stability of the whole product at risk.

2. Synchronized Delivery

In the world of microservices and distributed services, a single business process often spans multiple distinct services. Releasing a feature that requires updates to three different services simultaneously can be a logistical nightmare.

How it works:

  • Different teams (e.g., Development Team 1 and Team 2) work on their respective components independently.
  • Both teams wrap their new logic in toggle points that check the same feature flag.
  • Each component is deployed to production as "Latent Code" whenever it is ready.
  • Once all components are live and verified, you activate the single shared feature flag.

Why use it? This decouples deployment from release. Team 1 is not blocked by Team 2. Developers can plan their tasks better, and the feature is only "released" when the entire distributed system is ready to support it.

3. Direct Delivery

Sometimes you don't want to release a feature to everyone, but rather to a specific group of trusted users. This is the foundation of "Beta Testing" or "Internal Access."

How it works:

  • You build logic in your application to identify different sets of users.
  • In the Feature Flag service, you configure a direct shipment strategy.
  • You use identifier query parameters to map specific users or groups to the enabled feature.

Why use it? This is ideal for validating functionality in production with a controlled audience. You can grant access to your internal QA team or a friendly set of "Beta" customers to gather feedback before opening the floodgates to the general public.

4. Percentage Delivery

When you are confident in the feature but unsure about the load it might generate, or if you simply want to perform A/B testing, percentage delivery is the technique of choice.

How it works:

  • You release the feature to a specified percentage of your user base (e.g., 5% or 10%).
  • The service randomly (but consistently) assigns users to the "On" or "Off" group.
  • You can even manage Variations, where you deliver Implementation A to 10% of users and Implementation B to another 10% to see which performs better.

Why use it? This is the ultimate risk mitigation tool. If the new feature causes a performance regression, only a small fraction of your users are affected, and you can dial it back instantly. It also provides data-driven insights, allowing you to choose the implementation that receives the most positive feedback.

---

Example:

We start with our Shop Service (Service A). It is currently running in production and doing exactly what it was designed to do: it takes a user ID and calculates a checkout for the user. (Remember that we are here to talk about feature flag service, so for simplicity, the code developed in CAP is a very basic one).

The service.cds file looks like this:

service ShopService {
    // Define the structure of the response
    type CheckoutResponse {
        userId : String;
        finalPrice : Decimal;
        message : String;
    }

    // Function now returns the Object defined above
    function checkout(userId: String) returns CheckoutResponse;
}

The service.js file looks like this:

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = async function () {

    this.on('checkout', async (req) => {
        const { userId } = req.data;
        const randomAmount = Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000) + 1;

        return {
            userId: userId,
            finalPrice: randomAmount,
            message: `Checkout successful for user ${userId} with amount ${randomAmount}`
        };
    });
}

And when we hit the checkout endpoint, the following is retrieved:

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "1",
  "finalPrice": 538,
  "message": "Checkout successful for user 1 with amount 538"
}

Now, the business has introduced a new requirement: Loyalty Discounts. We want specific users to receive a percentage off their total bill. However, the logic for determining who is a VIP and how much discount they get is complex. It involves checking history, tier levels, and region.

To keep our architecture clean, we decided not to build this logic inside the Shop Service. Instead, we are creating a dedicated microservice: The Loyalty Service (Project B).

We are now facing a classic distributed system problem:

  1. Shop Service (A) needs to call Loyalty Service (B) to get the discount.
  2. The Loyalty Service is being developed by a different team (or is simply not ready yet).
  3. We cannot deploy the updated Shop Service until the Loyalty Service is live, or the Shop will crash when it tries to call a non-existent API.

This is the perfect use case for Latent Code Delivery.

We are going to update the Shop Service right now to include the orchestration logic. It will try to call the Loyalty Service. However, we will wrap that call in an SAP Feature Flag.

  • Flag OFF: The Shop Service acts exactly as it does today (Standard Price). It ignores the Loyalty Service completely.
  • Flag ON: The Shop Service calls the Loyalty Service, gets the discount, and calculates the new total.

This allows us to deploy the updated Shop Service today, even if the Loyalty Service doesn't exist yet. We are decoupled.

Now, let’s create a new feature flag:

 

With the flag created and disabled, now, let’s check the code being developed. In this implementation, we are modifying the existing checkout logic to support a new "Loyalty Discount" feature. However, we are wrapping this new logic in a Toggle Point so that we can control its release without redeploying the application.

We adapt the cds and js files:

service ShopService {
    // Define the structure of the response
    type CheckoutResponse {
        userId : String;
        originalAmount : Decimal;
        discountPercentage : Integer;
        finalPrice : Decimal;
        message : String;
    }

    // Function now returns the Object defined above
    function checkout(userId: String) returns CheckoutResponse;
}
const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = async function () {

    // 1. Connect to External Services
    const featureFlagService = await cds.connect.to('FeatureFlagService');
    const loyaltyService = await cds.connect.to('LoyaltyService'); // Connect to Service B

    this.on('checkout', async (req) => {
        const { userId } = req.data;

        // --- STANDARD LOGIC ---
        // 1. Calculate the base price (simulated random amount)
        let randomAmount = Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000) + 1;
        
        // 2. Default state (Standard Pricing)
        let finalPrice = randomAmount;
        let discountPercentage = 0;
        let message = "Standard price applied.";

        // --- THE TOGGLE POINT ---
        const flagName = 'loyalty-discount';
        let isFeatureEnabled = false;

        try {
            // Check the Flag status for this user
            const response = await featureFlagService.send({
                method: 'GET',
                path: `/evaluate/${flagName}?identifier=${userId}`
            });
            
            if (response && response.variation === 'true') {
                isFeatureEnabled = true;
            }
        } catch (error) {
            console.error(`[Feature Flag] Check failed: ${error.message}`);
        }

        // --- LATENT CODE BLOCK ---
        if (isFeatureEnabled) {
            console.log(`[Orchestration] Feature ON. Calling Loyalty Service for ${userId}...`);

            try {
                // >>> REAL CALL TO SERVICE B <<<
                // We ask Service B: "What is the discount for this user?"
                const loyaltyResponse = await loyaltyService.send({
                    method: 'GET',
                    path: `/getDiscount(userId='${userId}')`
                });

                // If Service B responds, we use ITS data
                if (loyaltyResponse && loyaltyResponse.percentage) {
                    discountPercentage = loyaltyResponse.percentage;
                    
                    finalPrice = randomAmount - (randomAmount * (discountPercentage / 100));
                    message = `Loyalty Applied! Service B gave you ${discountPercentage}% off.`;
                }

            } catch (error) {
                // FALLBACK: 
                // If Service B is down (or not created yet), we log it but don't crash.
                // This is crucial for "Synchronized Delivery".
                console.error(`[Orchestration] Failed to contact Loyalty Service: ${error.message}`);
                message = "Standard price applied (Loyalty Service unavailable).";
            }
        } else {
            console.log(`[Orchestration] Feature OFF. Skipping Loyalty Service call.`);
        }

        // --- FINAL RESPONSE ---
        return {
            userId: userId,
            originalAmount: randomAmount,
            discountPercentage: discountPercentage,
            finalPrice: parseFloat(finalPrice.toFixed(2)),
            message: message
        };
    });
}

At the very beginning, we establish connections to the two external services defined in our package.json.

  • FeatureFlagService: The service instance on SAP BTP Feature Flag Service that holds our flag configuration.
  • LoyaltyService: The new remote microservice (Project B) that calculates discounts.

Before executing any new logic, we ask the Feature Flag service if the loyalty-discount feature is active for the current user.

  • We send a GET request to the /evaluate endpoint, passing the userId as the identifier. This allows for Direct Delivery (e.g., enabling the feature only for specific users).
  • Notice the try/catch block around the flag check. If the Feature Flag service is down or unreachable, we catch the error and default isFeatureEnabled to false. This ensures the Shop Service never crashes just because the flag service is having a hiccup.

This is the core of the "Latent Code" delivery technique. The code inside the if (isFeatureEnabled) block is deployed to production but remains dormant until the flag is switched on.

  • If the flag is ON, we attempt to make a real HTTP call to the Loyalty Service.
  • We send the userId to Service B and wait for a calculated discount percentage.
  • If the Loyalty Service is down (or not even deployed yet), the catch block handles the error gracefully. The user simply gets the standard price, and the error is logged. This allows us to deploy Service A before Service B is ready.

Finally, the service returns the response.

  • If the flag was OFF, the user sees the originalAmount and a standard message.
  • If the flag was ON (and succeeded), the user sees the finalPrice and a loyalty message.

Now, with the flag off, we can see the following answer from our Shop Service.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "1",
  "originalAmount": 888,
  "discountPercentage": 0,
  "finalPrice": 888,
  "message": "Standard price applied."
}

If we enable our flag, it is expected that the process will fail, because service B is not developed yet. It is important that you enable the flags at the proper moment.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "1",
  "originalAmount": 220,
  "discountPercentage": 0,
  "finalPrice": 220,
  "message": "Standard price applied (Loyalty Service unavailable)."
}

Now, let’s develop Service B. In a separate project, we define the services and the logic.

service LoyaltyService {

    type DiscountResponse {
        userId : String;
        percentage : Integer;
        tier : String;
    }

    // The endpoint Service A will call
    function getDiscount(userId: String) returns DiscountResponse;
}
const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = async function () {

    this.on('getDiscount', async (req) => {
        const { userId } = req.data;

        // Default State
        let percentage = 0;
        let tier = 'Bronze';

        // Simulation Logic
        if (userId) {
            const id = userId.toLowerCase();

            if (id.startsWith('vip')) {
                percentage = 20;
                tier = 'Platinum';
            } else if (id.startsWith('a')) {
                percentage = 10;
                tier = 'Gold';
            }
        }

        console.log(`[Loyalty Service] Calculated discount for ${userId}: ${percentage}% (${tier})`);

        return {
            userId: userId,
            percentage: percentage,
            tier: tier
        };
    });
}

Now, after deploying our application, and knowing that we are confident enough to provide this new functionality to our end users, then we enable the feature flag.

After enabling it, and executing our checkout process, we can see that the new functionality is released.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip",
  "originalAmount": 889,
  "discountPercentage": 20,
  "finalPrice": 711.2,
  "message": "Loyalty Applied! Service B gave you 20% off."
}

Instantly—without restarting the Shop Service—the application behavior changes.

  1. Shop Service detects the flag is active.
  2. It opens the communication channel to Loyalty Service.
  3. Loyalty Service receives the request, identifies the user as a VIP, and returns a 20% discount.
  4. Shop Service calculates the final price and returns the "Loyalty Applied" message.

What we have just demonstrated is Synchronized Delivery.

In a real-world SAP BTP landscape, you might have the "Shop Team" and the "Loyalty Team" working on different sprints.

  1. The Shop Team deployed their code (with the flag OFF) on Monday.
  2. The Loyalty Team deployed their service on Wednesday.
  3. On Friday, the Product Owner clicked "Enable" in the dashboard.

Both services started working together instantly, with zero downtime and zero code rollbacks. This is the power of decoupling deployment from release.

Enhancing our rollout experience:

We have successfully orchestrated our services, but right now, the feature is either ON for everyone or OFF for everyone.

In a real-world scenario, you rarely roll out a major new feature to 100% of your users immediately. You want to test it with a trusted group first—perhaps your internal team or a set of "Beta" customers. This is where Direct Delivery comes in.

We don't need to change a single line of code to achieve this. Our code is already sending the identifier (userId) to the service.

So, let’s open our feature flag in our feature flag instance and let’s define a Direct Delivery Strategy, in which we define that only users vip01, and a01, will be able to experiment this new feature.

Now, if we try to apply a discount for vip02, it will not even reach/call service B, because the toggle is OFF for that user. Even if the Service B contemplates all kind of users that start with “vip”.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip02",
  "originalAmount": 498,
  "discountPercentage": 0,
  "finalPrice": 498,
  "message": "Standard price applied."
}
{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip01",
  "originalAmount": 445,
  "discountPercentage": 20,
  "finalPrice": 356,
  "message": "Loyalty Applied! Service B gave you 20% off."
}

You can also perform a percentage delivery. In my case, I have defined a 50/50 percentage, so the half of my users will take advantage of my new feature, meanwhile others will not be able to use it.

For example, vip06 is not being able to use this new feature, but vip33, can access to it.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip06",
  "originalAmount": 725,
  "discountPercentage": 0,
  "finalPrice": 725,
  "message": "Standard price applied."
}
{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip33",
  "originalAmount": 635,
  "discountPercentage": 20,
  "finalPrice": 508,
  "message": "Loyalty Applied! Service B gave you 20% off."
}

---

Personal Thoughts:

Let me share some personal thoughts from using the SAP Feature Flag Service and while redacting this blog post.

Regarding the setup and initial steps to get this service up and running. Consider that it is already available under the Build Code plan. This accelerates the process of incorporating it into your projects. The documentation is very accurate, and with the quick start guide, you can begin using the service in minutes. The only advice I can give, if you really want to take advantage of this service, is to take the time to understand its capabilities and how to adapt them to your needs. This is key to be fully aware of the benefits. The documentation is well-structured, is fast to read, and clearly explains many of the topics involved in using the service. You will find straightforward explanations about the different release methods, what you can achieve, how to perform the setup and how to maintain the feature flags.

Regarding the usage. I really think this is a very powerful tool. It helps developers and business experts agree on the release, coordinating efforts so everyone knows exactly when a feature is ready to go live. One of the most important aspects of releasing new features is the confidence of the project members prior to the launch of a new enhancement. In distributed systems, many factors must be considered during deployment. Even with unit tests, integration tests, and manual tests in place, there are always some concerns when releasing enhancements. Having flags that can enable and disable these features reduces those concerns significantly. By configuring the proper feature flags, you can test with end users before the official release. This ensures you get feedback on time and can improve the tool before everyone else sees it, increasing the confidence prior launch (and reducing possible issues in production).

Another important aspect that all project members should consider is the "next steps" after using these flags. Be careful, having too many blocks inside conditional statements can lead to complicated code (and nesting feature flags can also lead to complicated debugging sessions). Hence, the decommissioning of feature flags is an important step in the enhancement lifecycle. In my opinion, removing the flags in the code becomes a mandatory task if you want to keep your project clean and maintainable. So, do not underestimate the cleanup steps after releasing a new enhancement with this service.

---

Wrap Up:

We have journeyed from a simple, standalone "Shop Service" to a sophisticated, distributed system that orchestrates pricing logic with a "Loyalty Service."

By introducing the SAP Feature Flag Service, we didn't just add a toggle; we fundamentally changed how we deliver software.

  • Decoupling: We deployed Service A's orchestration logic before Service B was even ready.
  • Safety: We ensured that if Service B fails, Service A degrades gracefully to standard pricing.
  • Targeting: With Direct Delivery and Perncetage Delivery, we rolled out the new feature exclusively to a set of users, testing in production with zero risk to the end users.

This is the essence of modern cloud-native development on SAP Build Code. It is not just about writing code; it is about controlling when and how that code provides value to your users.

The SAP Feature Flag Service has even more depth, including complex rule sets and other capabilities that can be check in the following guide. If you find this topic interesting, let me know in the comments, and we can dedicate a future "Deep Dive" post to check more capabilities.

I hope you find this post useful.

Kind regards,

Nata.

entry.title: Convert Excel file to CSV in SAP Cloud Integration without External Libraries or JARs [Apache POI]
entry.content:
In many SAP Integration Suite (CPI) scenarios, we receive Excel files from SFTP or other channels and need to process them further — often by converting them into CSV. However, SAP CPI does not support Apache POI out of the box, and adding external libraries is not always feasible.

so, there's a way out, writing my first blog hope i didn't make much mistakes.

🎯 Convert XLSX file to CSV in SAP CPI Using Groovy (No External Libraries)

In this blog, I’ll show how to read an XLSX file, extract a specific worksheet by name, and convert it into CSV using pure Groovy, leveraging the fact that XLSX files are ZIP archives.

🧩Key Highlights of This Approach

  •  No Apache POI or external libraries

  • Works with payloads as (byte[], InputStream, or String)

  • Sheet selection by sheet name.

🛠️How the Solution Works (High Level)

  1. Read the incoming payload safely as byte[]

  2. Treat the XLSX file as a ZIP archive

  3. Extract:

    • workbook.xml → map sheet name to sheet ID

    • sharedStrings.xml → resolve string values

    • sheetX.xml → actual worksheet data

  4. Identify the correct worksheet by sheet name

  5. Convert rows and cells into CSV format

  6. Set CSV as the message body

🧪Groovy Script

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.util.zip.ZipInputStream
import java.io.*

def Message processData(Message message) {

    // =====================================================
    // CONFIG – CHANGE SHEET NAME HERE ONLY
    // =====================================================
    final String TARGET_SHEET_NAME = "Sales- Input"   // <-- change if needed

    // --- 1. Safely read payload as byte[] (SFTP safe) ---
    def body = message.getBody()
    byte[] xlsx

    if (body instanceof byte[]) {
        xlsx = body
    } else if (body instanceof InputStream) {
        xlsx = body.bytes
    } else if (body instanceof String) {
        xlsx = body.getBytes("UTF-8")
    } else {
        throw new Exception("Unsupported payload type: ${body?.getClass()}")
    }

    if (!xlsx || xlsx.length == 0) {
        throw new Exception("Excel file is empty")
    }

    // --- 2. Open XLSX (ZIP) ---
    def sharedStrings = []
    Map<String, String> sheetNameToId = [:]
    Map<String, byte[]> sheetFiles = [:]

    InputStream sheetStream = null

    def zip = new ZipInputStream(new ByteArrayInputStream(xlsx))
    def entry

    while ((entry = zip.nextEntry) != null) {

        def baos = new ByteArrayOutputStream()
        byte[] buffer = new byte[4096]
        int len
        while ((len = zip.read(buffer)) > 0) {
            baos.write(buffer, 0, len)
        }

        def contentBytes = baos.toByteArray()

        // ---- workbook.xml (sheet name → rId) ----
        if (entry.name == "xl/workbook.xml") {
            def wb = new XmlSlurper().parse(new ByteArrayInputStream(contentBytes))
            wb.sheets.sheet.each {
                sheetNameToId[it.@name.text()] = it.@'r:id'.text()
            }
        }

        // ---- sharedStrings.xml ----
        else if (entry.name == "xl/sharedStrings.xml") {
            def xml = new XmlSlurper().parse(new ByteArrayInputStream(contentBytes))
            xml.si.each { si ->
                if (si.t) {
                    sharedStrings << si.t.text()
                } else {
                    sharedStrings << si.r.collect { it.t.text() }.join('')
                }
            }
        }

        // ---- worksheets ----
        else if (entry.name.startsWith("xl/worksheets/sheet")) {
            def sheetFile = entry.name.replace("xl/worksheets/", "")
            sheetFiles[sheetFile] = contentBytes
        }
    }
    zip.close()

    // --- Resolve correct worksheet ---
    def relId = sheetNameToId[TARGET_SHEET_NAME]
    if (!relId) {
        throw new Exception("Sheet '${TARGET_SHEET_NAME}' not found")
    }

    int sheetIndex = relId.replace("rId", "").toInteger()
    def sheetFileName = "sheet${sheetIndex}.xml"

    if (!sheetFiles[sheetFileName]) {
        throw new Exception("Worksheet file ${sheetFileName} not found")
    }

    sheetStream = new ByteArrayInputStream(sheetFiles[sheetFileName])

    // --- 3. Convert sheet to CSV (UNCHANGED) ---
    def sheetXml = new XmlSlurper().parse(sheetStream)
    def csv = new StringBuilder()

    sheetXml.sheetData.row.each { row ->
        def line = row.c.collect { cell ->
            def raw = cell.v?.text() ?: ''
            def value = (cell.@t == 's' && raw) ? sharedStrings[raw.toInteger()] : raw

            if (value.contains(',') || value.contains('"') || value.contains('\n')) {
                "\"${value.replace('"', '""')}\""
            } else {
                value
            }
        }.join(',')

        csv.append(line).append('\n')
    }

    // --- 4. Output ---
    message.setBody(csv.toString())
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "text/csv")
    return message
}

Note *Make sure to change the sheet name accordingly (not excel file name).

🚧Adapter Compatibility

This Groovy script is independent of the sender adapter and works with SFTP, HTTP, and AS2, as long as the incoming payload is the raw XLSX file (binary or InputStream).

For HTTP senders, ensure the Excel file is not Base64-encoded and not wrapped inside multipart form-data with additional fields.

⚠️Limitations & Notes

  • This solution handles:

    • Strings
    • Numeric values
  • It does not handle:
    • Formulas
    • Styles
    • Dates with formatting
    • Large Excel files may impact performance (DOM parsing)

✅Conclusion

This Groovy-based solution provides a simple and dependency-free way to convert Excel files into CSV inside SAP CPI.

If you are dealing with Excel files from SFTP and need quick processing without external libraries, this approach can save a lot of effort.

💡 What’s Next?
This solution handles direct XLSX payloads efficiently.
With small enhancements, the same approach can be extended to support Base64 payloads from HTTP senders, multipart uploads, and runtime-configurable sheet names.

hope it helped you...🙂

entry.title: Reading Excel and Displaying Data in SAPUI5 Tables – Static vs Dynamic Columns
entry.content:
Hi,  

While working on SAPUI5 application, i came across a common business requirement where user needed to upload excel files and review the data before further processing  

SAPUI5 does not provide built in support for reading excel files on the client side, so i explored a client side solution that allows users to upload an excel, reads content and display it in the UI 

Introduction
 In many UI5 applications there will be a common scenario to allow users to upload Excel files and review the data before proceeding with further business process. since SAPUI5 does not provide built in support for reading excel files, developers need to rely on external libraries. So in this blog i will read excel data using xlsx library and display it in the SAPUI5 table.

Pre-requisites 

  1. Basic knowledge of SAPUI5 
  1. Sapui5 project setup 
  1. Xlsx.js file should be added in compinent.js file 

 

Component.js file 

var jQueryScript = document.createElement('script');
                jQueryScript.setAttribute('src', 'https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/xlsx/0.10.0/jszip.js');
                document.head.appendChild(jQueryScript);
           
                var jQueryScript = document.createElement('script');
                jQueryScript.setAttribute('src', 'https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/xlsx/0.10.0/xlsx.js');
                document.head.appendChild(jQueryScript);

What we are going to do here: 

  1. Upload excel file using FileUploader  
  1. Read the file using javascript fileReader 
  1. Excel file will be parsed using XLSX library 
  1. Extract the sheet from workbook 
  1. Will convert sheet data into json format 
  1. We will set json data to JSON Model 
  1. We will bind json model to table in the UI 

Note: the column names in the excel should be same as the properties used in the SAPUI5 table 

 

Controller.js File 

  
sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel",
    "sap/m/Column",
    "sap/m/Text",
    "sap/m/ColumnListItem"
],
    /**
     *  {typeof sap.ui.core.mvc.Controller} Controller
     */
    function (Controller, MessageToast, JSONModel, Column, Text, ColumnListItem) {
        "use strict";

        return Controller.extend("project1.controller.View1", {
            onInit: function () {

            },
            
            onUpload:function(oEvent){
                debugger
               var file =  oEvent.getParameter("files")[0]
                var that = this
                var reader = new FileReader()
                    reader.onload= function(e){
                        debugger
                        var data = e.target.result;
                        var workbook = XLSX.read(data, { type: "binary" });

                        // Take first sheet
                        var sheetName = workbook.SheetNames[0];
                        var sheet = workbook.Sheets[sheetName];

                        // Convert to JSON
                        var jsonData = XLSX.utils.sheet_to_json(sheet);

                        // Get your existing model from manifest.json
                        var oModel = that.getView().getModel("empModel");

                        // Replace empDetails with Excel data
                        oModel.setProperty("/empDetails", jsonData);
                    }
                    reader.readAsBinaryString(file);

            },

        });
    });

 

XML View 

 

<mvc:View controllerName="project1.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc" displayBlock="true"
    xmlns:unified="sap.ui.unified"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <content>

             <unified:FileUploader fileType="XLSX,xlsx" change="onUpload"  id="FileUploaderId" sameFilenameAllowed="true" iconOnly="false" buttonOnly="true" icon="sap-icon://upload" iconFirst="true" style="Emphasized" >
              </unified:FileUploader>
              <!-- <Button text="Upload" press="onPress" ></Button> -->

            <Table items="{empModel>/empDetails}">
                <columns>
                    <Column>
                        <Text text="Name" />
                    </Column>
                    <Column>
                        <Text text="Location" />
                    </Column>
                    <Column >
                        <Text text="Age"/>
                    </Column>
                    <Column >
                        <Text text="Salary"/>
                    </Column>
                    <Column >
                        <Text text="EmployeeID"/>
                    </Column>
                    <Column >
                        <Text text="Nationality"/>
                    </Column>
                </columns>
                <items>
                    <ColumnListItem>
                        <cells>
                            <Text text="{empModel>Name}"></Text>
                            <Text text="{empModel>Location}"></Text>
                            <Text text="{empModel>Age}"></Text>
                            <Text text="{empModel>Salary}"></Text>
                            <Text text="{empModel>EmployeeID}"></Text>
                            <Text text="{empModel>Nationality}"></Text>
                        </cells>
                    </ColumnListItem>
                </items>
            </Table>


            <!-- <Table id="idTable" inset="false"/>  -->

        </content>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

Output 

 

Dynamic excel upload and table generation in UI5 

In above approach, that solution works only when the excel file contains fixed and known column names 

In real scenarios, excels may have different structure, may have different column names, and may have different number of rows 

So to handle that situation 

  • Read excel data dynamically 
  • Extract columns headers from excel 
  • Will create UI5 columns at run-time 
  • Then bind table rows dynamically 

Controller.js file  

Step1: 

 

  • Fetch the uploaded excel file 
  • Initializes Filereader to read file content 

Step2: 

  • Conver binary excel data into a workbook object 
     

Step3: 

  • Always reads the first sheet from excel 

Step4: 

  • Converts excel rows into json objects 
  • Default -> ensures empty cells are not skipped 
     

Step5: 

  • Extract cloumns names from excel first row 
  • These headers will used to create table columns 

Step6: 

  • Clears the previously created columns before adding new ones 
     

Step7: 

  • Iterated through headers 
  • Creates Coulmns dynamically for each excel columns 
     

Step8: 

  • Stores excel data in the model 
  • Enables data binding to table rows 
     

Step9: 

  • Creates cells dynamically  
  • Each cell will bind to corresponding excel fields 
     

Step10: 

  • Binds excel rows to table 
  • Tables renders automatically with dynamic structure 

 Controller Code 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel",
    "sap/m/Column",
    "sap/m/Text",
    "sap/m/ColumnListItem"
],
    /**
     *  {typeof sap.ui.core.mvc.Controller} Controller
     */
    function (Controller, MessageToast, JSONModel, Column, Text, ColumnListItem) {
        "use strict";

        return Controller.extend("project1.controller.View1", {
            onInit: function () {

            },
        onUpload: function (oEvent) {
                debugger
                var file = oEvent.getParameter("files")[0];
                var that = this;
                var reader = new FileReader();

                reader.onload = function (e) {
                    debugger
                        var data = e.target.result;
                        var workbook = XLSX.read(data, { type: "binary" });

                        // Take first sheet
                        var sheetName = workbook.SheetNames[0];
                        var sheet = workbook.Sheets[sheetName];

                        // Convert to JSON with empty values for blank cells
                        var jsonData = XLSX.utils.sheet_to_json(sheet, { defval: "" });

                        // Extract headers dynamically
                        var headers = Object.keys(jsonData[0]);

                        // Get table reference
                        var oTable = that.byId("idTable");
                        oTable.removeAllColumns();

                        // Build columns dynamically
                        headers.forEach(function (header) {
                            oTable.addColumn(new sap.m.Column({
                                header: new sap.m.Text({ text: header })
                            }));
                        });

                        var oModel = that.getView().getModel("empModel");
                        oModel.setProperty("/empDetails", jsonData);

                        // Create template row
                        var oTemplate = new sap.m.ColumnListItem({
                            cells: headers.map(function (header) {
                                return new sap.m.Text({ text: "{empModel>" + header + "}" });
                            })
                        });

                        // Bind items
                        oTable.bindItems({
                            path: "empModel>/empDetails",
                            template: oTemplate
                        });

                };
                reader.readAsBinaryString(file);
        }

        });
    });

 

XML View  

<mvc:View controllerName="project1.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc" displayBlock="true"
    xmlns:unified="sap.ui.unified"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <content>

             <unified:FileUploader fileType="XLSX,xlsx" change="onUpload"  id="FileUploaderId" sameFilenameAllowed="true" iconOnly="false" buttonOnly="true" icon="sap-icon://upload" iconFirst="true" style="Emphasized" >
              </unified:FileUploader>
           
            <Table id="idTable" inset="false"/> 

        </content>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

Output 

 

Conclusion

in this blog we discussed how to upload and read excel files in SAPUI5 application using a client side approach. We covered both fixed column excel handling and dynamic solution that adapts varying excel structures

entry.title: Re: LMS Onboarding Assignment Profile Rule
entry.content:
Hi Mel

You can use a simple rule 'Hire Date On or After 1/1/1900' (1900 is the oldest year you can select I believe but you can of course choose something more recent, as long as it captures all hire dates).

This will capture every newly synced user, and if an employee changes Hire Date for any reason, the AP won't impact their assignments in unwanted ways.

Hope this helps.

Kostas

entry.title: Re: Signavio Intelligence TrafficProblem
entry.content:
Hi @HandeAkca , thank you for your post. To investigate further in detail, it seems, you need to follow the process via Signavio Product Support with the component BPI-SIG-PI-DM. We asked the support and they stated that they are not aware of the issue via their ticketing system. 

So,  please create a support case using SAP for Me (log in with S-User ID and password). 

 

You will need to select the specific SAP Signavio product / component: 
System: 
Search for SAP Signavio  
Product Area: 
SAP Signavio Process Manager > SAP Signavio Process Manager 
Component: 
BPI-SIG-PI-DM
 
The responsible team will then take care.

entry.title: Equity Pickup
entry.content:
Hi.

We have configured Equity Pickup according to the guides with a tick in Read Group Currency as we want the amounts to be read and the postings to be done in Group Currency that is the local currency for the parent company

The group is not performing currency translation task as the group currency value is taken direct from Accounting (Currency Translation method S0907).

When I run Equity Pickup the job fails with an error message saying 

Enter rate DKK / CHF rate type 100* for 31.05.2025 in the system settings
 
However, entering a rate for 31.05.2025 would not make sense as the retained earnings for the period would not match a calculation based on a single day's exchange rate.
 
Is it not possible to run Equity Pickup when using Currency Translation Method S0907 or do we need to take other things into considerations when we do the Equity Pickup configuration?
 
Br.
 
Jan

entry.title: Re: Using SuccessFactors Onboarding for Country Transfers Without Recruiting
entry.content:
@prasad1315 

When the internal person applies and we initiate onboarding the system with do the rehire check and create rehire check with a new employment.  If there is not a termination record in the current employment that is before the new hire date then the button showing 'Rehire with New Employment' in step 3 will not appear.  As soon as you enter in the termination date the button will appear as the screen is dynamic.  Does this answer your question?

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Connector - websocket connection in SAP clo...
entry.content:
It should be possible, as SCC supports WebSocket communication. For this you need to define a system mapping for protocol HTTPS and then in the access control settings expose the "entry point URL" for the WebSocket upgrade and mark the checkbox "Upgrade Allowed" in the "Add Resource" screen.

See "Limit the Accessible Services for HTTP(S)" in Configure Access Control (HTTP) | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Re: Datasphere: Hiding dimensions used for filtering from...
entry.content:
Hi @mitko1994 ,

You are absolutely correct. If you want to filter on a column in an AM, that column will be visible when consuming the model. 

However, there is a way to get around it. 

  1. Create an Analytical Model (AM1) and place a filter on 0MAT_SALES__VENDOR.  Do not remove any fields. 
  2. Create a second Analytical Model (AM2) and use AM1 as a source. 

In AM2, you can freely remove the columns you don't want to expose to your end-user. 

Yes, it is clunky as hell, but it'll work 🙂

entry.title: Re: Nominations for learning program / initiative
entry.content:
Hi Sam

From the sounds of it, you could create these first as a Program - though standalone Items can also work - then publish them in a Library that an Assignment Profile will make available only to the employees you want to reach out to (you can also make them available to everyone).

You can allow users to self-register to the Program or Items, and you can also allow Managers to assign it to their Direct or Indirect reports.

Finally, you can create a Direct Link for a Directed Library Search and use it either in a Banner or via email or any way that would work for your organisation to "advertise" it.

Hopefully this helps as a high level concept.

Kostas
